danger_signals.visual_form: Unspecified hostile entities in each stage environment
danger_signals.meaning: Collision with enemies reduces health by one heart
All hearts lost triggers level restart
Time expiration causes immediate game over and complete level loss

danger_signals.visual_form: Switchblade Sam as final stage antagonist
danger_signals.meaning: Boss encounter represents primary threat to town safety

safety_signals.visual_form: Collection of five large coins per stage
safety_signals.meaning: Progress toward victory condition and stage completion
Rescue objectives that contribute to winning condition

safety_signals.visual_form: Mr. Wilson's coin collection throughout levels
safety_signals.meaning: Recovery of property indicates world restoration progress

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Arrives via railroad connection to town
Mr. Wilson's house, outdoor town areas

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Switchblade Sam accessible as boss encounter; enemies present in stages
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must progress through four sequential stages to confront antagonist and rescue civilians

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Mr. Wilson's coin collection scattered and unavailable in natural locations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Mandatory collection objective requires systematic stage traversal to recover stolen assets

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Joey and Margaret positioned as rescue objectives within levels
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player locates and retrieves both friends as part of victory condition

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Four distinct stage settings: Mr. Wilson's house, great outdoors, boiler room, boss chamber
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Each environment contains thematically appropriate enemies and collectibles

environmental_storytelling.example: Mr. Wilson's house as opening stage establishes burglar entry point and neighborhood setting
Coin collection distributed across stages communicates theft and dispersal of property
Sequence from residential (house) → natural (outdoors) → industrial (boiler room) → confrontation (final boss) creates narrative escalation
Joey and Margaret placement within dangerous stages visually communicates kidnapping situation
Railroad connection detail establishes how external threat infiltrated peaceful town

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Temporary stun effect and loss of one health heart
Level restart with retained collected coins
Immediate game over; complete stage progress loss
Patrolling/stationary threat that triggers contact damage
Final stage confrontation as culmination threat

notes: Dennis the Menace SNES lacks detailed visual communication documentation in available sources. The Wikipedia article focuses on gameplay mechanics rather than visual manifestation patterns. World-state communication inferred from game structure (four stages with escalating threat, protagonist journey from home through outdoors to industrial setting toward final confrontation). The game represents a straightforward rescue/recovery narrative with danger indicated through health system and time pressure mechanics rather than sophisticated visual semiotics. Environmental storytelling relies on stage selection and objective placement rather than dynamic visual indicators. No documented faction color schemes, detailed enemy visual variants, or sophisticated environmental hazard signals are available from sources.
